languages.bornNative: Happy Birthday — the day you were born

stateLang.state: Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu

stateFormation.from: Andhra State (carved out of Madras State 1-Oct-1953) merged with the Telugu districts of Hyderabad State; born in coastal Andhra before Oct 1953 = born in Madras State. Lost Telangana on 2-Jun-2014
was NEFA (North-East Frontier Agency), administered as part of Assam until 1972, then a Union Territory
Assam Province of British India; since gave away Nagaland (1963), Meghalaya (1972), Mizoram and Arunachal (1972 as UTs)
Bihar Province; its entire southern half became Jharkhand on 15-Nov-2000
carved out of Madhya Pradesh
Portuguese territory until 19-Dec-1961, then part of the UT of Goa, Daman and Diu; born in Goa before Dec 1961 = born under Portuguese rule
carved out of bilingual Bombay State; born in Ahmedabad before May 1960 = born in Bombay State
carved out of Punjab
a Union Territory (earlier Part C state of merged hill princely states); gained Kangra and other hill areas of Punjab on 1-Nov-1966
carved out of Bihar
unified Mysore State — old Mysore princely state plus Kannada areas of Bombay, Hyderabad and Madras States and Coorg
merger of Travancore-Cochin with the Malabar district of Madras State; born in Kozhikode before Nov 1956 = born in Madras State
merger of old Madhya Pradesh (ex Central Provinces and Berar), Madhya Bharat, Vindhya Pradesh and Bhopal State; lost Chhattisgarh on 1-Nov-2000
carved out of bilingual Bombay State; born in Bombay before May 1960 = born in Bombay State
princely kingdom until Oct 1949, then a Part C state / Union Territory
carved out of Assam (autonomous state within Assam from 2-Apr-1970); born in Shillong before 1972 = born in Assam's capital
was the Lushai Hills / Mizo Hills district of Assam until 1972, then a Union Territory
carved out of Assam (Naga Hills district and Tuensang Area)
Orissa Province of British India; spelling anglicised as Orissa for 61 years of the republic
trifurcation of the larger East Punjab (which had absorbed PEPSU in 1956) — Haryana split off, hill areas went to Himachal, Chandigarh became a UT
union of the Rajputana princely states (integrated 1949); took current boundaries when Ajmer State merged in on 1-Nov-1956
independent Himalayan kingdom under the Chogyal (an Indian protectorate); born in Gangtok before May 1975 = not born in India at all
Madras Presidency became Madras State; Telugu districts left for Andhra State on 1-Oct-1953, Malabar left and Kanyakumari joined on 1-Nov-1956
carved out of Andhra Pradesh; anyone born in Hyderabad 1956–2014 was born in Andhra Pradesh — and before 1-Nov-1956, in Hyderabad State
princely kingdom until Oct 1949, then a Part C state / Union Territory
was the United Provinces of British India, renamed two days before the republic; lost its hill districts to Uttarakhand on 9-Nov-2000
carved out of Uttar Pradesh as Uttaranchal
the Indian half of partitioned Bengal Province; absorbed Cooch Behar (1950) and French Chandernagore (1954)
a Part D territory of the new republic, made a Union Territory by the States Reorganisation Act
carved out of Punjab as a Union Territory to serve as shared capital of Punjab and Haryana

stateFormation.state: Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu
stateFormation.from: merger of two UTs — Dadra & Nagar Haveli (freed from Portugal 1954, UT from 11-Aug-1961) and Daman & Diu (Portuguese until Dec 1961, part of Goa UT until 30-May-1987)
Part C state until 1956, then a plain Union Territory; became the National Capital Territory with its own assembly under the 69th Amendment
was a full state with special status under Article 370 for the entire 1950–2010 window; downgraded to a Union Territory and split from Ladakh
was the Leh and Kargil districts of Jammu and Kashmir state; anyone born there 1950–2010 was born in J&K
detached from Madras State as the UT of Laccadive, Minicoy and Amindivi Islands
French India — de facto handover 1-Nov-1954, de jure only on 16-Aug-1962; born there before Nov 1954 = born in France's India

eras.headline: Shyamchi Aai (1953) wins India's first Golden Lotus
eras.story: Acharya Atre's Shyamchi Aai took the very first National Film Award President's Gold Medal, and Anant Mane's Sangtye Aika (1959) closed the decade by making Jayshree Gadkar the queen of tamasha cinema.

eras.headline: Tamasha fever: Ek Gaon Bara Bhangadi (1968)
eras.story: Lavani ruled the screen — Jayshree Gadkar dazzled in Sawaal Majha Aika! (1964) and Anant Mane's Ek Gaon Bara Bhangadi (1968), with Nilu Phule's sly villagers stealing scenes.

eras.headline: Pinjra (1972) — melodrama meets the new wave
eras.story: V. Shantaram's Pinjra broke records while Shantata! Court Chalu Aahe (1971) and Jabbar Patel's Saamna (1975, invited to Berlin) launched Marathi parallel cinema; Dada Kondke's Songadya comedies packed rural talkies.

eras.headline: Ashi Hi Banwa Banwi (1988) — the comedy cult
eras.story: Ashok Saraf, Laxmikant Berde and Sachin turned Marathi film into a laughter factory with Ashi Hi Banwa Banwi and Dhumdhadaka (1985), while Jabbar Patel's Umbartha (1982) gave Smita Patil a landmark.

eras.headline: Maherchi Sadi (1991) weeps its way into history
eras.story: Vijay Kondke's tearjerker with Alka Kubal ran 75 weeks and became the biggest Marathi hit ever to that point; Doghi (1995) and Tu Tithe Mee (1998) kept serious drama alive.

eras.headline: Shwaas (2004) sends Marathi cinema to the Oscars
eras.story: Sandeep Sawant's Shwaas won the Golden Lotus and became India's Oscar entry, igniting a renaissance sealed by Jogwa (2009) and Harishchandrachi Factory (2009).

eras.headline: Bhangra (1959) sets Punjab dancing
eras.story: A post-Partition industry rebuilt itself on comedies like Posti (1950), Lachhi and Madari, until Jugal Kishore's Bhangra, with Sunder and Nishi and Mohammed Rafi on playback, made bhangra songs a screen staple.

eras.headline: Nanak Naam Jahaz Hai (1969) — the miracle hit
eras.story: Released for Guru Nanak's 500th birth anniversary, it ran for months and single-handedly revived Punjabi cinema; Satluj De Kandhe (1964) had earlier won the National Award.

eras.headline: The devotional decade
eras.story: Nanak Dukhiya Sub Sansar (1970) and Dukh Bhanjan Tera Naam (1974) rode the devotional wave, while Mehar Mittal's comedy and rustic romances seeded the masala style of the decade to come.

eras.headline: Chann Pardesi (1980) wins national respect
eras.story: The National Award-winning Chann Pardesi, with Raj Babbar and Amrish Puri, opened a golden run — Gurdas Maan lit up Long Da Lishkara (1986) and Marhi Da Deeva (1989) brought literary gravitas.

eras.headline: Shaheed-e-Mohabbat Boota Singh (1999)
eras.story: In a decade of near-collapse, Gurdas Maan's Partition love story won the National Award and toured world festivals — the lifeline that kept Punjabi cinema breathing.

eras.headline: Jee Aayan Nu (2002) — the NRI revival
eras.story: Manmohan Singh's Harbhajan Mann starrers Jee Aayan Nu and Asa Nu Maan Watna Da (2004) rebooted the industry for the diaspora era, with Mel Karade Rabba (2010) passing the baton to a new generation.

eras.headline: Malela Jeev (1956) and the family 'socials'
eras.story: Manhar Raskapur filmed Pannalal Patel's Malela Jeev with the novelist himself scripting — heir to Gunsundari and Kariyavar, these social dramas of home and marriage carried a lean decade.

eras.headline: Mehndi Rang Lagyo (1960)
eras.story: The musical hit Mehndi Rang Lagyo and Akhand Saubhagyavati (1963, with Asha Parekh) gave Gujarati cinema its pan-family blockbusters and songs still sung at weddings.

eras.headline: Jesal Toral (1971) opens the golden age
eras.story: Ravindra Dave's saint-outlaw legend was a monster hit, and Upendra Trivedi became the decade's superstar with folk hits like Santu Rangili (1976) and Kashino Dikro (1979).

eras.headline: Bhavni Bhavai (1980) meets the Kanodia wave
eras.story: Ketan Mehta's folk-theatre landmark with Naseeruddin Shah won national honours, while Naresh Kanodia's musical melodramas like Dhola Maru (1983) ruled the single screens.

eras.headline: Desh Re Joya Dada Pardes Joya (1998) smashes every record
eras.story: Govindbhai Patel's NRI family saga earned around Rs 22 crore on Rs 10 tickets — the biggest Gujarati hit of the century; Manvini Bhavai (1993) brought Upendra Trivedi National Award glory.

eras.headline: Vikram Thakor and the village blockbuster
eras.story: As urban screens faded, Vikram Thakor's rural romances like Ek Var Piyu Ne Malva Aavje (2006) filled heartland cinemas, keeping Dhollywood alive until the urban reboot of the 2010s.

eras.headline: Kedar Gouri (1954) — poetry on celluloid
eras.story: Nitai Palit filmed Radhanath Rai's tragic romance with music by Bhuban-Kishan, while Sri Jagannath (1950) and Bhai Bhai (1956) moved Odia film from mythology toward social themes.

eras.headline: Matira Manisha (1966) — Mrinal Sen in Odia
eras.story: Sri Lokenath (1960) won Odia cinema's first National Award, and Mrinal Sen's Matira Manisha turned Kalindi Charan Panigrahi's novel into a classic of the soil.

eras.headline: Shesha Shrabana (1976) and the Prashanta Nanda storm
eras.story: Actor-director Prashanta Nanda's blockbusters swept both box office and National Awards — Shesha Shrabana ran riot and was later remade by Nanda himself in Hindi as Naiyya.

eras.headline: Maya Miriga (1984) travels the world
eras.story: Nirad Mohapatra's joint-family elegy won international festival acclaim, while the star pair Uttam Mohanty and Aparajita ruled the Odia mainstream.

eras.headline: Siddhanta Mahapatra's action decade
eras.story: Masala vehicles made Siddhanta Mahapatra Ollywood's box-office king, while art films like Indradhanura Chhai (1995) carried Odisha to the festival circuit.

eras.headline: I Love You (2004) crowns Anubhav Mohanty
eras.story: Hara Patnaik's romance ran 100 days across Odisha, made debutant Anubhav Mohanty a superstar and set off the remake-era boom of Ollywood romances.

eras.headline: Piyoli Phukan (1955) wins the first National honour
eras.story: Phani Sarma's freedom-fighter epic, scored by a young Bhupen Hazarika, won the President's medal; Hazarika then directed Era Bator Sur (1956), and Puberun (1959) travelled to Berlin.

eras.headline: Shakuntala (1961) — Bhupen Hazarika's milestone
eras.story: Hazarika's award-winning musical brought partial colour to Assamese film, and Maniram Dewan (1963) gave the decade its historical epic and the immortal song 'Buku Hom Hom Kore'.

eras.headline: Chameli Memsaab (1975)
eras.story: Abdul Majid's tea-garden romance became a landmark blockbuster and won Bhupen Hazarika the National Award for music — its songs still drift across Assam.

eras.headline: Halodhia Choraye Baodhan Khai (1987) wins the Golden Lotus
eras.story: Jahnu Barua's farmer's tale swept the National Awards and Locarno, while Bhabendra Nath Saikia's Agnisnan (1985) made this the critical peak of Assamese cinema.

eras.headline: Xagoroloi Bohu Door (1995)
eras.story: Jahnu Barua's boatman elegy toured world festivals and Firingoti (1992) won national honours — art cinema kept the flag flying through a dark decade for the industry.

eras.headline: Hiya Diya Niya (2000) revives the box office
eras.story: Munin Barua's blockbuster made Jatin Bora a superstar and debuted Zubeen Garg as composer; Zubeen's own Mon Jai (2008) sealed the pop-cinema comeback.

eras.headline: Ganga Maiyya Tohe Piyari Chadhaibo (1963) — a cinema is born
eras.story: Kundan Kumar's devotional drama, premiered in Patna in honour of President Rajendra Prasad, launched Bhojpuri cinema; Bidesiya (1963) and Loha Singh (1966) followed fast.

eras.headline: Dangal (1977) brings colour
eras.story: After a long lull, Dangal — the first colour Bhojpuri film — reignited the heartland circuit, and Balam Pardesia (1979) packed the single screens of Bihar and eastern UP.

eras.headline: Ganga Kinare Mora Gaon (1983)
eras.story: The second wave — Ganga Kinare Mora Gaon, Bitia Bhail Sayan and Hamar Bhauji drew huge rural crowds before the boom faded at decade's end.

eras.headline: Sasura Bada Paisawala (2004) — the great revival
eras.story: Manoj Tiwari's mega-hit out-grossed Bollywood across its belt; Saiyyan Hamar (2001) had made Ravi Kishan a star, and Panditji Batai Na Biyah Kab Hoi (2005) confirmed the boom.
